textPlain: This title had the garden path sentence effect on me.https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Garden-path_sentence

Seems they've figured out how to wiggle themselves forward using their tails, then land in a way that creates a suction between their belly and the rocks. Clever.https://www.researchgate.net/publication/394405635_Bumblebee...
this dude made an aquarium with an artificial mini waterfall for the fish to climb https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qp0SIJNtIKM
Caveman brain:  "That'd be a great spot to sit and just pick up a few fish for lunch."
"the catfish adhered to the wet rock by creating a pressurized air bubble under their belly."Presumably they mean negatively pressurized?
Me, trying to ship in a waterfall organization

I am unreasonably upset by the tiktok-goofy-jazz music they chose.For science.org I want something a bit more nature-documentary e.g. a thoughtful classical/ambient soundscape with David Attenborough gentle tones "And here we see...". If seeking to amuse me then go for it e.g. Ride of Valkeries/Rohirrim.

The original title is plural: "Thousands of climbing catfish filmed scaling waterfalls"The word "catfish" can be singular or plural. I read it as "A catfish was filmed...". But there sure are a lot of those little fish.

> ...researchers also found that the [wet-rock-climbing] bumblebee catfish isn’t alone. Three other fish species were also scaling the waterfalls alongside the bumblebee catfish, and none of the four had ever been documented climbing before.Being fish, this sounds like convergent evolution.  (Vs. learned behaviors.)
Huh, interesting. Doesn't happen much in fusional languages.
That's kind of what brown bears do with salmon migrating up the stream / jumping out of the water.
Catfish is disgusting to eat, at least the ones we have here in Spain, which are sometimes past 50kg.

I found the music a great fit and I would not have enjoyed it as much without. I Would have closed the window if it had been Ride of the Valkyries which is far more generic and overplayed for me than anything tiktok, but I have never used tiktok.

They seem to all be fish that live in fairly fast flowing waters, my guess was they are able to use rocks+suction system to hold in place for stuff, they probably don't even really have a concept they are climbing a wall, it could just feel like a particularly intense rapid?
